required_decoration_zones: FOREHEAD: vermillion tikka between the antlers
ANTLERS: gold-leaf accent along the tines with folk-band rings at base
NECK: folk collar in indigo + leaf-green with white throat patch rendered as a folk-cream panel

BODY: the WHITE SPOTS preserved as a rhythmic ornament — each spot a small white circle with vermillion or saffron folk-dot center, arranged in horizontal rows along the back and flanks; the spots are themselves the primary ornament
WHITE BELLY: preserved as a cream folk panel with vermillion border at the body-belly seam
FOUR LEG ANKLETS: vermillion + saffron folk bands
TAIL: short with white folk-cream underside visible in motion

anatomical_count_constraints.antlers: 2 branched with 3 tines each (males) or no antlers (females)

animals.conservation_note: Bhitarkanika's mangrove edges and grassland margins host a healthy chital population, which is the primary prey base for the park's tigers (occasionally visiting from Similipal) and the resident saltwater crocodiles at the waterline.

animals.signature_features: large stork (1.1m tall) with NEARLY BALD YELLOWISH-PINK HEAD AND NECK (sparse downy feathers, almost vulture-like), MASSIVE YELLOW DAGGER BILL, dark slate-gray-to-black back and wings, WHITE BELLY and white underwing, long pink-orange legs. The bald yellowish head + huge dagger bill + dark-and-white body is the unmistakable adjutant silhouette.
animals.signature_action: standing tall with neck stretched up and bill pointed skyward, wings half-spread, drying in the sun after a swamp feeding
animals.rest_pose_for_species: hunched on a tree branch with neck retracted into shoulders, bill tucked downward, long legs visible below
animals.anatomy_must_include: NEARLY BALD YELLOWISH-PINK HEAD AND NECK (mandatory diagnostic — NOT feathered like a heron, NOT crested like a sarus crane); MASSIVE YELLOW DAGGER BILL (proportional to head, must look heavy); DARK back and wings WITH WHITE BELLY (the contrast is diagnostic); LONG PINK-ORANGE LEGS; both feet visible; the result must read as LESSER ADJUTANT (a stork, not a vulture, not a crane).
required_decoration_zones: HEAD + NECK: the bald yellowish-pink skin rendered as a folk-cream panel with sparse small folk-dot 'down' marks; small vermillion accent on the bill base
BILL: massive yellow dagger rendered with gold-leaf folk accent
BREAST + BACK (dark zone): the dark back rendered as deep-indigo folk panels with leaf-vein motifs in subtler tones, anchored by the body's primary indigo fill
WHITE BELLY: preserved as a cream folk panel with vermillion border at the body-belly seam
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + cream
LEGS: long pink-orange legs rendered as folk-vermillion panels with cream ankle bands
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (set in bald skin)
anatomical_count_constraints.head_feathering: nearly bald (mandatory — NOT a feathered head)

animals.conservation_note: Bhitarkanika's mangrove and adjacent wetland complex is one of the most important breeding sites for the lesser adjutant in eastern India. Population threatened by wetland loss and human disturbance at nesting trees.

animals.signature_features: large coastal raptor with PURE WHITE HEAD, BREAST, BELLY, AND UNDERWING contrasting with SLATE-GRAY back and upper wings, SHORT WHITE TAIL with a black band at the base (visible only in flight), heavy hooked yellow-and-gray bill, yellow legs with massive black talons. The clear WHITE-AND-GRAY two-tone is the unmistakable adult diagnostic.
animals.signature_action: diving toward water with wings half-folded, talons extended forward, body angled steeply, prey targeted below
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a high mangrove branch with wings folded, white head turned to scan, body upright and alert
animals.anatomy_must_include: PURE WHITE HEAD, BREAST, BELLY (mandatory — not pale gray, not buff); SLATE-GRAY BACK AND UPPER WINGS (mandatory contrast); HEAVY HOOKED YELLOW-AND-GRAY BILL; YELLOW LEGS with MASSIVE BLACK TALONS; the white-and-gray two-tone must be SHARP not gradient; the result must read as ADULT WHITE-BELLIED SEA EAGLE (not a juvenile, not a fish eagle, not a brahminy kite).
required_decoration_zones: HEAD (white zone): the pure white head preserved as a cream folk panel with vermillion eye ring and small folk-dot at the bill base
BILL: heavy hooked yellow-and-gray bill with gold-leaf folk accent along the cere
BREAST + BELLY (white zone): preserved as a cream folk panel with minimal vermillion + saffron rosette accents at the chest center — do NOT overdecorate the white
BACK + UPPER WINGS (gray zone): slate-gray rendered as a deeper indigo folk panel with leaf-vein motifs and folk feather bars
TAIL: short white with a black band at base, rendered as a folk-bar pattern
LEGS + TALONS: yellow legs with vermillion + saffron ankle bands; massive black talons rendered with bold folk-black outline
EYE: vermillion eye ring around the dark yellow iris

anatomical_count_constraints.tail: 1 short white with black base band
anatomical_count_constraints.talons: massive black hooked claws visible on visible foot

animals.conservation_note: Apex coastal raptor of Bhitarkanika's mangrove-estuarine ecosystem; pair-bonds for life and returns to the same tall nest tree for decades. Indicator species for healthy mangrove tidal systems.

animals.signature_features: broad raptor with distinct HOODED CREST of black-and-white feathers at the back of the head (mandatory diagnostic — gives the species its name), BRIGHT YELLOW BARE SKIN around eye and to bill (the cere), broad dark-brown wings, WHITE-SPOTTED BLACK UNDERWINGS visible in flight, white-and-dark-banded broad tail, pale belly with brown speckles.
animals.signature_action: perched in profile with crest fully raised and yellow facial skin prominent, head turned to scan, talons gripping the branch
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly on a branch with crest lowered, both feet visible, wings folded, head turned slightly
animals.anatomy_must_include: HOODED CREST at back of head (mandatory — never bare-headed); BRIGHT YELLOW FACIAL SKIN extending from eye to bill base; broad dark-brown wings; white spots on the dark underwings visible in flight pose; banded tail; both feet visible; the result must read as CRESTED SERPENT EAGLE not generic eagle or hawk.
required_decoration_zones: CREST: black-and-white folk-feather crest with leaf-vein motif at the tips
FACE: yellow facial-skin panel preserved as a folk-gold panel with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BELLY: folk-dot pattern in indigo + saffron, the brown speckles rendered as rhythmic ornament dots
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + cream with WHITE SPOTS preserved as a rhythmic ornament of small folk-cream circles each with a vermillion dot center
TAIL: folk-bar pattern alternating indigo and cream bands
TALONS: yellow legs rendered as folk-gold panels with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: One of the most commonly seen raptors of Jim Corbett — a snake-and-reptile specialist whose presence indicates a healthy small-vertebrate prey base. Vocal in flight with a distinctive whistling call.

animals.signature_features: massive black hornbill with WHITE NECK AND BREAST, WHITE BANDS on wing and tail, and the unmistakable MASSIVE BRIGHT YELLOW CASQUE atop a curved yellow-and-black bill (the casque is THE diagnostic — large, two-pointed at the front in mature birds, never absent). Long heavy bill, yellow facial skin around eye.
animals.signature_action: in flight with broad black-and-white wings fully spread, the white wing-bar conspicuous, casque held forward, long tail streaming
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a tall canopy branch with body upright, casque prominent, both feet gripping the branch, tail draped
animals.anatomy_must_include: MASSIVE YELLOW CASQUE on top of bill (mandatory size — proportional but unmistakably large, two-pointed at front); WHITE NECK contrasting with black body; WHITE BANDS on dark wings and tail; long curved yellow-and-black bill; both feet visible on branch; the result must read as GREAT HORNBILL not malabar-pied-hornbill (which has different white pattern) and not generic hornbill.
required_decoration_zones: CASQUE: massive yellow casque rendered with gold-leaf folk accent, two-pointed front with leaf-vein motifs
BILL: long curved yellow-and-black bill with folk-pattern bands
NECK (white zone): preserved as folk-cream panel with vermillion border
BREAST + BODY (black-teal zone): forest-teal body fill with folk panels in saffron + leaf-green at the chest and flank
WINGS: folk feather bars with WHITE WING BANDS preserved as cream stripes flanked by vermillion
TAIL: folk-bar pattern with WHITE TAIL BAND preserved
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (in yellow facial skin)
anatomical_count_constraints.wings: 1 visible in profile (with white wing band)

animals.conservation_note: Jim Corbett's mature sal-and-mixed forests harbor a small but stable Great Hornbill population at the western edge of the species' range; a strong indicator of old-growth canopy health.

animals.signature_features: male is a striking BLACK-AND-WHITE bustard — JET-BLACK HEAD, NECK, AND UNDERPARTS contrasting with PURE-WHITE WINGS that are dazzling in display flight; long legs; small head with no crest. Female is drab brown-streaked for camouflage in grass. Among the rarest bustards on Earth (~1000 mature individuals globally).
animals.signature_action: male's courtship display: leaping vertical from grass with white wings flashing fully open, body extended upward, then floating back down — the iconic 'parachute' display
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing alert in tall grass with body vertical, head erect, legs visible, wings folded close
animals.anatomy_must_include: MALE: JET-BLACK head/neck/underparts (mandatory contrast); PURE-WHITE WINGS visible in display (mandatory — the white-on-black is THE diagnostic); long legs; small unmarked head (no crest, no plume); the result must read as BENGAL FLORICAN (a bustard, not a heron, not a crane).
required_decoration_zones: HEAD + NECK (black zone): preserved with minimal vermillion folk accent only at eye ring; the black is anchor
BREAST + BODY (black zone): folk panels in muted saffron + vermillion at chest center only — do not overdecorate the black
WINGS (white zone): preserved as folk-cream panels with leaf-vein motifs and vermillion accent at wing tips and primary tips
BACK: folk-stripe pattern in indigo + cream where the wing meets the body
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern
LEGS: long folk-yellow panels with vermillion ankle bands
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: One of the world's rarest bustards (~1000 mature globally). Kaziranga's tall grassland — and adjacent Manas — are the most important breeding strongholds in India; population continues to decline due to grassland conversion.

animals.signature_features: stocky brown partridge-like bird with finely BLACK-AND-WHITE-STREAKED back and flanks, CHESTNUT THROAT PATCH with a fine WHITE THROAT LINE bordering it (the unmistakable diagnostic), pale buff belly, red-orange legs, prominent dark eye stripe. Cryptic in marshland grass.
animals.signature_action: running through tall wetland grass with body lowered, head forward, wings tucked
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing on a mud bank with body horizontal, head turned, both feet visible
animals.anatomy_must_include: CHESTNUT THROAT PATCH with WHITE THROAT LINE bordering it (mandatory combination — never plain throat); fine BLACK-AND-WHITE STREAKING on back and flanks (not solid color); red-orange legs (not yellow, not pink); the result must read as SWAMP FRANCOLIN specifically, not a generic partridge or gray francolin.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN + EYE STRIPE: folk-stripe pattern in indigo + cream with the prominent eye-stripe rendered as a folk-line in vermillion + black
THROAT: chestnut patch with white throat line preserved as a folk-cream stripe bordered by vermillion folk-band
BREAST + FLANKS: the streaked plumage translated as parallel folk-stripes in indigo + cream + saffron
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron with leaf-vein motifs
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern
LEGS: red-orange rendered as vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (with dark eye stripe)

animals.conservation_note: Kaziranga's floodplain grassland is one of the world's most important habitats for this specialist; the species depends entirely on wet grassland that has been lost across most of its historical range.

animals.signature_features: black-and-white hornbill with WHITE BELLY and WHITE TAIL TIPS contrasting against the dark body, distinctive BLACK CASQUE (smaller than the great hornbill's, with a black ridge on top of the yellow bill), bare blue facial skin around eye, modest tail. Mid-sized hornbill — smaller than great-hornbill, larger than grey hornbills.
animals.signature_action: perched on a fruiting tree branch with bill open mid-feed, fig held in bill tip
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly with bill held forward, both feet visible, tail draped behind
animals.anatomy_must_include: BLACK CASQUE on top of yellow bill (mandatory — small ridge-like, NOT massive yellow like great-hornbill); WHITE BELLY and WHITE TAIL TIPS (mandatory contrast); BLUE BARE FACIAL SKIN around eye; mid-sized body; the result must read as MALABAR PIED HORNBILL not great-hornbill (larger yellow casque) and not generic hornbill.
required_decoration_zones: CASQUE: small black ridge on yellow bill, rendered with folk-pattern bands
BILL: long yellow bill with folk-pattern accent bars
FACE: blue facial-skin patch preserved as folk-indigo panel around vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BACK (black-teal zone): forest-teal body fill with folk panels in saffron + leaf-green at the chest center
WHITE BELLY: preserved as folk-cream panel with vermillion border at the body-belly seam
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron
TAIL: folk-bar pattern with WHITE TAIL TIPS preserved as cream banding
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (in blue facial skin)
anatomical_count_constraints.casque: 1 small black ridge (NOT massive yellow like great-hornbill)

animals.conservation_note: Bandhavgarh hosts a healthy population in its mature sal forests; the species depends on large old hollow trees for nesting and is among the most easily seen hornbills in central India.

animals.signature_features: wild ancestor of the domestic chicken: male has BRIGHT GOLDEN-ORANGE NECK HACKLES cascading down, LONG GREEN-BLUE METALLIC TAIL FEATHERS (the iconic sickle tail with curving central feathers), bright RED COMB AND WATTLES on the head, slate-blue legs, red bare facial skin. Female is drab brown for camouflage. NEVER as gaudy as a domestic rooster — wilder and more athletic.
animals.signature_action: male in display: head thrown back with comb erect, golden hackles fanned out, tail arched high with sickle feathers prominent
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing on the forest floor with body horizontal, head turned, golden hackles draped, tail held normally
animals.anatomy_must_include: BRIGHT GOLDEN-ORANGE NECK HACKLES (mandatory — never solid red, never absent); LONG CURVING GREEN-BLUE METALLIC SICKLE TAIL (mandatory in males); RED COMB AND WATTLES (proportional, not exaggerated); slate-blue legs (not yellow domestic-rooster legs); the result must read as WILD RED JUNGLE FOWL (athletic, sleeker than domestic) not as a barnyard rooster.
required_decoration_zones: COMB + WATTLES: vermillion folk panels with small saffron dots, on the head and throat
GOLDEN HACKLES: cascading folk-pattern hackles rendered as parallel saffron + vermillion + gold-leaf strands flowing from nape down to shoulders
FACE: red facial skin folk-panel with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BODY: folk panels with leaf-vein motifs in indigo + saffron
WINGS: folk feather bars with metallic-green-and-indigo alternation
SICKLE TAIL: the long curving sickle feathers rendered with metallic green-blue folk-color rings, the central pair particularly prominent
LEGS: slate-blue rendered as folk-indigo panels with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (in red facial skin)

animals.conservation_note: Bandhavgarh's sal forests harbor pure wild-type Red Junglefowl — the genetic ancestor of every domestic chicken on Earth. Populations elsewhere are increasingly hybridized with feral chickens; central India's protected populations remain critical.

animals.signature_features: spectacular blue-toned songbird with PALE-BROWN BACK and HEAD, LILAC THROAT AND BREAST, BRIGHT TURQUOISE BELLY, and brilliantly BLUE-AND-AZURE WINGS that flash dazzling in flight (the 'roller' display shows brilliant primary-blue bands when wings spread). Strong straight black bill, dark eye, short tail.
animals.signature_action: in 'roller' aerial display with wings fully spread showing the brilliant turquoise-and-azure flight pattern, body angled, tail fanned
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched alertly on a low fence or wire with wings folded, the lilac and brown body subtle but the eye sharp
animals.anatomy_must_include: PALE-BROWN BACK AND HEAD (not solid blue) contrasting with LILAC THROAT and TURQUOISE BELLY (mandatory color-band on the body); BRILLIANT BLUE WINGS in two shades (azure and turquoise) visible in spread-wing pose; strong straight bill; the result must read as INDIAN ROLLER specifically (a sit-and-wait insectivore with the iconic flight display) not a kingfisher and not a generic blue songbird.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN + BACK (pale-brown zone): folk-pattern panel with leaf-vein motifs in indigo + saffron
THROAT (lilac zone): folk-panel in vermillion + saffron with rhythmic dot accents
BREAST + BELLY (turquoise zone): folk-panel in turquoise-and-indigo with leaf-vein motifs
WINGS (the showpiece): folk feather bars in alternating AZURE and TURQUOISE with the primary-feather pattern preserved as a brilliant blue-band design
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern in indigo with turquoise tips
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.wings: 1 visible in profile (in display: spread showing azure + turquoise)

animals.conservation_note: Kanha's open meadows and edge habitat are prime Indian Roller territory. The state bird of several Indian states (Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, Telangana); among India's most-photographed songbirds for its dazzling flight display.

animals.signature_features: all-dark ibis with a RED BARE-SKIN PATCH on the BACK OF THE HEAD (the unmistakable diagnostic — visible only from behind / side angles), a long DOWN-CURVED BLACK BILL, slate-black body with a WHITE SHOULDER PATCH visible at rest, dark legs. Among the largest ibises in India.
animals.signature_action: wading through shallow water with long curved bill probing the mud, head down, body horizontal
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing in a wet meadow with head turned to show the red nape patch, bill lowered, white shoulder patch visible
animals.anatomy_must_include: RED BARE-SKIN PATCH on back of head (mandatory diagnostic — without it the bird reads as a glossy ibis or another dark wader); LONG DOWN-CURVED BLACK BILL (mandatory ibis shape); WHITE SHOULDER PATCH visible against the dark body; dark legs (not red, not pink); the result must read as RED-NAPED IBIS specifically, not glossy ibis (which lacks the red nape patch).
required_decoration_zones: CROWN + NAPE: the red nape patch preserved as a folk-vermillion panel with saffron border
FACE: dark face with folk-line eye accent
BILL: long down-curved black bill with folk-pattern bands
BREAST + BODY: deep-indigo body with folk panels in saffron + leaf-green at chest center
WHITE SHOULDER PATCH: preserved as a folk-cream panel with vermillion border
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + leaf-green
LEGS: long dark legs rendered as folk-indigo panels with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: Kanha's meadow-edge wetlands harbor reliable ibis populations. Unlike its more cosmopolitan glossy-ibis relative, the Red-naped Ibis is endemic to the South Asian floodplain wetlands and is a specialist of seasonal pools and rice-field margins.

animals.signature_features: the 'jewel of the forest floor' — short-tailed plump songbird with a startling MULTI-COLOR palette: BUFF CROWN bordered by BLACK CROWN STRIPES, WHITE THROAT, GREEN BACK, BRILLIANT BLUE WING PATCH AND BLUE TAIL, BRIGHT RED VENT (under the tail — the 'wow' splash of color), pink legs, dark eye line. Every feather group is a different bold color.
animals.signature_action: hopping on the leaf litter with body upright, tail flicking up to flash the red vent below
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing on a low branch or root with body upright, head turned, tail held normally
animals.anatomy_must_include: BUFF CROWN with BLACK BORDER STRIPES (mandatory pattern); WHITE THROAT; GREEN BACK; BRILLIANT BLUE WING PATCH (mandatory); BRIGHT RED VENT (the iconic under-tail red — mandatory, do not omit); pink legs; the multi-color combination is THE diagnostic — the result must read as INDIAN PITTA, never a flycatcher, never a thrush.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN (buff zone) + CROWN STRIPES: folk-cream crown with bold black border stripes rendered as folk-line ornament
FACE: dark eye line preserved as folk-line in black
THROAT (white): folk-cream panel with vermillion border at the gorge line
BREAST + BELLY: folk-buff panel with leaf-vein motifs in indigo
WINGS (blue patch — the showpiece): folk-pattern blue panel in azure + turquoise with leaf-vein motifs
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in leaf-green
RED VENT (the under-tail red): preserved as a bright vermillion folk-panel under the tail — mandatory and prominent
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (with dark eye line)

animals.conservation_note: A migratory species that breeds in the central Indian forests including Pench, then moves to peninsular and Sri Lankan wintering grounds. Its call (a distinctive whistled 'wheet-tieu') is the soundtrack of pre-monsoon central-India forests.

animals.signature_features: medium-large raptor with PROMINENT POINTED CREST at the back of the head (mandatory diagnostic), brown above with WHITE BELLY heavily STREAKED in dark brown (the streaking is rhythmic and dense), broad rounded wings, long barred tail, yellow legs and feet, dark hooked bill. Variable coloration (dark and pale morphs) gave it the 'changeable' name.
animals.signature_action: perched on a tall sal-tree branch with crest fully raised, head turned to scan, talons gripping the branch firmly, wings folded
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly on a branch with crest lowered, both feet visible, wings folded, body upright
animals.anatomy_must_include: PROMINENT POINTED CREST (mandatory — never absent); BROWN UPPER, WHITE-WITH-DARK-STREAKS UNDER (mandatory streak pattern); BROAD ROUNDED WINGS visible in profile; LONG BARRED TAIL; yellow legs; the result must read as CRESTED HAWK-EAGLE (the crest + streaked belly is THE diagnostic) not generic eagle, not serpent-eagle.
required_decoration_zones: CREST: pointed folk-feather crest with leaf-vein motif
FACE: folk-line eye accent with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BELLY (white-streaked): the dense streaking translated as parallel folk-stripes in indigo + cream
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron
TAIL: long folk-bar pattern with alternating indigo and cream bands
FEET: yellow rendered as folk-gold panels with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: Apex avian predator of Pench's mature sal forest — preys on smaller mammals, junglefowl, and reptiles. Its presence indicates a balanced mid-canopy ecosystem.

animals.signature_features: large heavy owl with prominent LONG EAR-TUFTS (the 'horns' that give the genus its name — mandatory diagnostic), BRIGHT ORANGE-YELLOW EYES set in a buff facial disc, BROWN-AND-BUFF STREAKED plumage on body, brown-streaked belly with fine dark streaks, feathered legs and feet, strong dark hooked bill. Among the largest owls of the Indian subcontinent.
animals.signature_action: perched on a rocky ledge at dusk with ear-tufts fully raised, orange eyes wide and forward-facing, head turned to scan
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly with ear-tufts laid back, eyes half-closed, body upright on a ledge
animals.anatomy_must_include: LONG EAR-TUFTS (mandatory — these are the 'horns' that define the species; never absent); BRIGHT ORANGE-YELLOW EYES set forward in the facial disc (mandatory color); BROWN-AND-BUFF streaked body; feathered legs; the result must read as INDIAN EAGLE-OWL (large + horned + orange-eyed) not a brown wood-owl, not a barn owl.
required_decoration_zones: EAR-TUFTS: long folk-feather tufts with leaf-vein motifs in indigo + saffron
FACE (the disc): buff facial disc preserved as folk-cream panel with BRIGHT ORANGE-YELLOW EYES emphasized as folk-gold rounds (mandatory color)
BILL: dark hooked bill with folk-pattern accent
BREAST + BELLY: the streaked plumage translated as parallel folk-stripes in indigo + cream
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron
FEATHERED LEGS: feathered foot rendered as folk-pattern with vermillion claw tips
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 2 (both forward — owls have binocular vision; mandatory pair)

animals.conservation_note: Tadoba's rocky ridges and dry-deciduous forest provide ideal Indian Eagle-Owl habitat. A nocturnal apex predator that takes prey up to the size of small monitor lizards and peafowl.

animals.signature_features: male in white phase is unmistakable — JET-BLACK CRESTED HEAD with bright blue facial skin around eye, WHITE BODY with fine black shaft-streaks, and TWO VERY LONG WHITE TAIL STREAMERS (roughly twice the body length — the defining feature). Rufous phase exists with chestnut body. The long streaming tail is the visual signature whether in white or rufous phase.
animals.signature_action: in mid-flight with the long white tail streamers trailing behind in a graceful S-curve, wings spread, body angled
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a low branch with tail streamers draped down, crest raised, head turned
animals.anatomy_must_include: BLACK CRESTED HEAD (mandatory crest); BLUE FACIAL SKIN around eye; TWO VERY LONG TAIL STREAMERS (~2x body length — mandatory in adult males, the defining feature; never short, never absent); both feet visible; the result must read as PARADISE FLYCATCHER specifically (the trailing streamers are the unmistakable diagnostic) not a flycatcher of any other genus.
required_decoration_zones: CREST + HEAD (black zone): folk-black crest with leaf-vein motif, preserved as anchor
FACE: blue facial-skin patch preserved as folk-indigo panel around vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BODY (white zone): preserved as folk-cream panel with fine vermillion + saffron shaft-streak accents
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo with cream stripe accents

VERY LONG TAIL STREAMERS (the showpiece): the two long white streamers rendered with folk-cream banding and small leaf-vein flourishes along their length — the LENGTH is the defining feature, never truncate them
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (in blue facial skin)
anatomical_count_constraints.tail_streamers: 2 VERY LONG (mandatory in adult male — must be ~2x body length)

animals.conservation_note: A summer breeding migrant to central Indian forests including Tadoba. The trailing streamers are visible only in mature males that have completed their multi-year plumage transition. The state bird of Madhya Pradesh.

animals.signature_features: large mangrove-specialist kingfisher with MASSIVE BRIGHT-CORAL-RED DAGGER BILL (the dominant feature — disproportionately large), CHESTNUT-AND-BROWN PLUMAGE across head, breast, and underparts, DARK BROWN WINGS (the namesake), pale buff belly, BRILLIANT TURQUOISE RUMP visible in flight only, dark eye. Sundarbans endemic — one of the largest kingfishers in Asia.
animals.signature_action: perched motionless on a mangrove root with the massive red bill held forward, eye fixed on water below
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly on a horizontal mangrove root with bill held forward, body upright, both feet gripping
animals.anatomy_must_include: MASSIVE BRIGHT-CORAL-RED DAGGER BILL (mandatory — disproportionately large relative to head, the unmistakable diagnostic); CHESTNUT HEAD AND UNDERPARTS; DARK BROWN WINGS; the bill-to-body ratio is THE defining feature; the result must read as BROWN-WINGED KINGFISHER (the largest of Indian kingfishers, a mangrove specialist) not stork-billed kingfisher (which has a different chestnut pattern).
required_decoration_zones: MASSIVE BILL (the showpiece): the disproportionately-large coral-red dagger bill rendered with folk-vermillion + saffron accents and a gold-leaf tip — the size is mandatory and is the visual anchor
HEAD + BREAST (chestnut zone): folk-pattern panel in saffron + vermillion with leaf-vein motifs
BACK + WINGS (brown zone): folk feather bars in indigo + saffron
BUFF BELLY: preserved as folk-cream panel with vermillion border
RUMP (the hidden turquoise): visible only in flight pose — folk-turquoise patch as flash accent
FEET: both visible (red-orange) with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: Sundarbans is one of the most important strongholds for this mangrove-dependent species globally; population threatened by mangrove loss and rising salinity in the delta.

animals.signature_features: cryptic secretive waterbird with OLIVE-GREEN BACK and head, BLACK FACE MASK around the bill (the unmistakable diagnostic — extends from the bill to behind the eye), WHITE THROAT contrasting sharply with the black mask, BUFF UNDERPARTS, BARE YELLOW SKIN PATCH around eye, LOBED YELLOW-GREEN FEET (lobed not webbed — distinctive among waterbirds), long neck and slim body.
animals.signature_action: swimming low in mangrove channel with neck stretched forward, body half-submerged, eye fixed warily on the bank
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched motionless on a low mangrove root with body horizontal, neck retracted, lobed feet gripping
animals.anatomy_must_include: BLACK FACE MASK around bill extending to behind eye (mandatory diagnostic — without it the bird reads as a generic waterbird); WHITE THROAT (mandatory contrast with the black mask); OLIVE-GREEN body; LOBED YELLOW-GREEN FEET (mandatory — never webbed-duck-like, never plain claws); the result must read as MASKED FINFOOT, one of the world's rarest and most cryptic waterbirds.
required_decoration_zones: FACE MASK (black zone): preserved as a folk-indigo panel from bill back to behind the eye, with vermillion eye ring set inside
THROAT (white zone): preserved as folk-cream panel with vermillion border
CROWN + BACK (olive-green): folk-pattern panel with leaf-vein motifs in indigo + leaf-green
BREAST + BODY (buff zone): folk panel in saffron + cream with rhythmic dot accents
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + leaf-green
LOBED FEET (the diagnostic): the unusual lobed feet rendered with folk-pattern showing distinct foot-lobe segments — emphasize the unique structure
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern
animals.conservation_note: One of the world's rarest waterbirds (<1000 mature individuals globally). Sundarbans is the most important remaining stronghold in India; the species has declined sharply across mainland Asia due to mangrove conversion.

animals.signature_features: medium-large eagle with slim athletic build, DARK BROWN UPPERPARTS with a paler PALE BUFF NAPE, WHITE-STREAKED PALE UNDERPARTS, distinctive DARK BAND along the trailing edge of the wing (visible in flight), long broad-banded tail with a dark terminal band, yellow legs and feet. More slender and athletic-looking than spotted eagles.
animals.signature_action: soaring with wings held flat in classic hunting circle, dark wing-trailing-edge visible against pale underside
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a rocky pinnacle with wings folded, head turned to scan, both feet visible
animals.anatomy_must_include: PALE BUFF NAPE contrasting with the dark crown (mandatory); WHITE-STREAKED PALE UNDERPARTS (mandatory streak pattern); DARK BAND on wing trailing edge (visible in flight, mandatory diagnostic); LONG TAIL with broad bars and dark terminal band; SLIM ATHLETIC silhouette (NOT bulky like a spotted eagle); the result must read as BONELLI'S EAGLE, distinct from other Aquila species.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN (dark) + NAPE (pale buff): folk panel with the pale nape as a folk-cream highlight
FACE: folk-line eye accent with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BELLY (pale streaked): the dense streaking translated as parallel folk-stripes in indigo + cream
BACK + WINGS (dark): folk feather bars in indigo + saffron with the trailing-edge dark band preserved as a folk-black border
TAIL: long folk-bar pattern with broad bands and a folk-black terminal band
FEET: yellow rendered as folk-gold panels with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.wings: 1 visible in profile (with dark trailing edge)

animals.conservation_note: Gir's dry-deciduous-thorn-scrub habitat is prime Bonelli's Eagle territory. A specialist hunter of medium-sized mammals and game birds, declining globally due to habitat loss in Mediterranean and South Asian range.

animals.signature_features: small dark eagle, ALL-DARK-BROWN PLUMAGE with a paler buff nape patch, faint pale spotting on wing coverts (juveniles have more spotting), YELLOW GAPE (the corners of the mouth) visible against the dark face, broad rounded wings, broad short tail with fine barring, yellow legs. Smaller and dumpier than the Greater Spotted Eagle.
animals.signature_action: soaring low over scrubland with wings held in a shallow V, broad rounded wings prominent
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a low thorn-scrub branch with wings folded, both feet visible, head lowered
animals.anatomy_must_include: ALL-DARK-BROWN PLUMAGE (NOT pale, NOT streaked — uniformly dark); FAINT PALE SPOTTING on wing coverts (mandatory but subtle); YELLOW GAPE visible at mouth corners (mandatory diagnostic to separate from greater spotted eagle); BROAD ROUNDED WINGS; broad short tail; the result must read as INDIAN SPOTTED EAGLE specifically (a South Asian endemic) not greater spotted eagle.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN + NAPE: folk-dark panel with paler buff nape as folk-cream highlight
FACE: yellow gape preserved as folk-gold panel with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BELLY (dark): folk panel in indigo with subtle leaf-vein motifs — the dark is anchor
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron with FAINT PALE SPOTS preserved as small folk-cream circles
TAIL: broad folk-bar pattern with fine barring
FEET: yellow rendered as folk-gold panels with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: An endemic to the Indian subcontinent and one of the rarer Aquila eagles. Gir provides excellent dry-forest hunting habitat. Population threatened by habitat loss and persecution.

animals.signature_features: Western Ghats endemic hornbill, smaller than great-hornbill, with PLAIN GREY-BROWN PLUMAGE (no bold black-and-white pattern), modest small RIDGE-LIKE CASQUE on a YELLOW-AND-ORANGE BILL, dark eye, pale belly, short rounded wings. Less spectacular than great-hornbill — its character is in the subtle palette and the elegant slim profile.
animals.signature_action: perched on a fig branch with bill held forward, head turned, body upright in classic hornbill posture
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly with bill lowered, both feet gripping, body upright
animals.anatomy_must_include: PLAIN GREY-BROWN BODY (NOT black-and-white patterned like Malabar Pied Hornbill); modest RIDGE-LIKE CASQUE (NOT a massive casque like great-hornbill — small, low-profile); YELLOW-AND-ORANGE BILL; dark eye; the result must read as MALABAR GREY HORNBILL specifically (the smaller, plainer Western Ghats endemic) not great-hornbill, not Indian-grey-hornbill (open country).
required_decoration_zones: CASQUE: small ridge-like casque rendered as folk-saffron + gold-leaf accent — must be modest, not massive
BILL: yellow-and-orange bill with folk-pattern bands
FACE: folk-line eye accent with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BODY (grey-teal zone): forest-teal body fill with folk panels in saffron + leaf-green at chest
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + leaf-green
TAIL: folk-bar pattern with subtle banding
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: Western Ghats endemic — endemic to the wet evergreen and moist deciduous forests of the southern Western Ghats. Periyar's mature canopy is prime habitat. A keystone seed-disperser for many tropical tree species.

animals.signature_features: cryptic nocturnal bird with bark-camouflage BROWN-AND-RUFOUS MOTTLED PLUMAGE (looks like dead leaves or a broken branch), LARGE YELLOW EYES (huge for the small body — mandatory diagnostic of nocturnal predator), WIDE FROG-LIKE GAPE (the namesake — extremely wide mouth visible when opened), low flat forehead, short stubby tail, drab unmarked legs. Master of forest-floor camouflage.
animals.signature_action: perched motionless on a branch with body angled at 45 degrees like a broken twig, eyes fully open and forward, mouth slightly agape revealing the wide gape
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched in vertical 'broken-branch' camouflage pose with eyes closed (during day), body tucked tight against bark
animals.anatomy_must_include: LARGE YELLOW EYES (mandatory size — disproportionately huge for the body); WIDE FROG-LIKE GAPE visible when mouth opens (mandatory diagnostic — the namesake); BARK-MOTTLED brown-and-rufous plumage (NOT solid color, NOT crisp pattern — looks like dead leaves); LOW FLAT FOREHEAD (NOT a domed head like an owl); the result must read as FROGMOUTH (not owl, not nightjar — frogmouths have wider mouths and rougher plumage than nightjars).
required_decoration_zones: FOREHEAD + CROWN: folk-mottled pattern with bark-vein motifs
EYES: LARGE YELLOW eyes preserved as folk-gold rounds with vermillion eye ring — the size is mandatory and is the visual anchor
WIDE GAPE: when mouth open, the wide pink-and-buff frog-mouth interior rendered as a folk-cream panel
BREAST + BELLY: bark-mottled pattern translated as rhythmic folk-stripe + leaf-vein motifs in indigo + saffron + cream
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo with bark-vein motif accents
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern
FEET: both small visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 2 LARGE YELLOW (mandatory — forward-facing, huge for body size)

animals.conservation_note: Endemic to the Western Ghats and Sri Lanka. Periyar's wet evergreen forest is prime habitat. Detected mostly by its distinctive nocturnal call — a frog-like throaty croak that gives the species its English name.

animals.signature_features: Bandipur population of the Crested Hawk-Eagle — same species as the Pench entry (#67) but rendered from the Western Ghats teak-forest population: PROMINENT POINTED CREST at the back of the head, brown above with WHITE BELLY heavily streaked dark brown, broad rounded wings, long barred tail, yellow legs.
animals.signature_action: soaring over Bandipur's open teak forest with broad wings held flat, crest pressed back by airflow, banded tail fanned
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a high teak-tree branch with crest fully raised, head turned to scan
animals.anatomy_must_include: POINTED CREST (mandatory); brown above with WHITE-STREAKED UNDERPARTS; broad rounded wings; long barred tail; yellow legs; the result must read as CRESTED HAWK-EAGLE — Bandipur variant matches Pench variant in body grammar but with renderer freedom to vary the rest-pose context (teak forest vs sal forest).
required_decoration_zones: CREST: pointed folk-feather crest with leaf-vein motif
FACE: folk-line eye accent with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BELLY (white-streaked): parallel folk-stripes in indigo + cream
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron
TAIL: long folk-bar pattern with alternating indigo and cream bands
FEET: yellow rendered as folk-gold panels with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: Common across Bandipur's deciduous teak and mixed forest. A generalist forest raptor that benefits from the contiguous Nilgiri Biosphere protected area.

animals.signature_features: stunning forest trogon, MALE has BRILLIANT CRIMSON-RED BREAST AND BELLY contrasting with BLACK HEAD AND THROAT, narrow WHITE COLLAR between the black throat and the red breast (the unmistakable diagnostic), OLIVE-BROWN BACK, FINELY BARRED BLACK-AND-WHITE WING COVERTS, blue eye ring. Female has dull cinnamon-brown plumage replacing the crimson and black.
animals.signature_action: perched motionless on a vertical understory branch with body upright, crimson breast facing forward, eye scanning for insects
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly on a low vertical branch with body upright, head turned, both feet gripping
animals.anatomy_must_include: MALE: BRILLIANT CRIMSON-RED BREAST AND BELLY (mandatory color saturation); BLACK HEAD AND THROAT (mandatory contrast); NARROW WHITE COLLAR between black throat and red breast (mandatory diagnostic — the white line is THE identifier); OLIVE-BROWN BACK; FINELY BARRED BLACK-AND-WHITE wing-covert pattern; the result must read as MALABAR TROGON specifically (the only trogon of peninsular India).
required_decoration_zones: HEAD + THROAT (black zone): folk-black panel preserved as anchor with subtle vermillion eye ring
WHITE COLLAR: narrow folk-cream stripe between throat and breast (mandatory diagnostic — never omit)
BREAST + BELLY (crimson zone): folk-vermillion panel with rhythmic dot accents in saffron
BACK + WINGS (olive-brown + finely barred): folk-pattern with the finely barred wing coverts translated as parallel folk-line ornament in indigo + cream
TAIL: folk-bar pattern with red-and-black banding
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (with blue eye ring)
anatomical_count_constraints.white_collar: 1 narrow stripe between black throat and red breast (mandatory)

animals.conservation_note: Western Ghats endemic; Bandipur's moist deciduous forest is at the dry edge of its range. The species is a vulnerable indicator of mature forest understory health — declines quickly where forests are degraded.

animals.signature_features: small owl with prominent SHORT EAR-TUFTS (smaller than eagle-owl's, but still mandatory), CRYPTIC GREY-BROWN BARK-MOTTLED PLUMAGE, BRIGHT ORANGE EYES set in pale facial disc, ROW OF WHITE SCAPULARS (white spots on the shoulders — diagnostic), short tail, feathered legs. Approximately the size of a large songbird — much smaller than the eagle-owl.
animals.signature_action: perched at the entrance of a tree hollow at dusk with ear-tufts raised, orange eyes wide and forward
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched compactly on a branch with ear-tufts laid back, eyes half-closed, body upright
animals.anatomy_must_include: SHORT EAR-TUFTS (mandatory — smaller than eagle-owl but present); BRIGHT ORANGE EYES (mandatory color); ROW OF WHITE SCAPULARS on shoulders (mandatory diagnostic — distinguishes from other small Asian owls); CRYPTIC GREY-BROWN BARK-MOTTLED plumage; SMALL body size; the result must read as SCOPS OWL (small, eared, orange-eyed) not eagle-owl, not barn owl.
required_decoration_zones: EAR-TUFTS: short folk-feather tufts with leaf-vein motifs
FACE (the disc): pale facial disc preserved as folk-cream panel with BRIGHT ORANGE EYES as folk-gold rounds (mandatory color)
WHITE SCAPULARS: the row of white shoulder spots preserved as a sequence of folk-cream circles each with vermillion border — mandatory diagnostic
BREAST + BELLY (bark-mottled): folk-stripe pattern in indigo + cream with leaf-vein motifs
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron
FEATHERED LEGS: feathered foot rendered as folk-pattern with small vermillion claw tips
animals.conservation_note: Anamalai's mixed deciduous-evergreen forest provides ideal nesting hollows for this small nocturnal predator. Its distinctive 'whuk' call (a single drawn-out hoot) is a familiar Western Ghats night sound.

animals.signature_features: all-black raptor with distinctively LONG FINGERED WINGS (the wings appear unusually long and slender with prominent 'finger' primary feathers), pale yellow CERE AND FEET, broad fan-shaped tail with finely barred bands, yellow legs, dark hooked bill, yellow eye. Glides extremely slowly over forest canopy — has the lowest wing-loading of any eagle.
animals.signature_action: gliding slowly across the canopy with very long wings outstretched and 'fingers' (primary feathers) splayed, body tilted on the slightest air current
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a high canopy snag with wings folded back showing the long primaries, both feet visible, body upright
animals.anatomy_must_include: ALL-BLACK PLUMAGE (mandatory — no streaks, no contrast); UNUSUALLY LONG FINGERED WINGS (mandatory — primary feathers prominent as 'fingers'); pale YELLOW CERE AND LEGS contrasting with the black body (mandatory color contrast); the result must read as BLACK EAGLE specifically (a canopy-glide specialist), distinguished by the all-black body + yellow accents + long wings.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN + FACE (black zone): folk-black panel preserved with vermillion eye ring around yellow iris
BREAST + BODY (black zone): the all-black body anchored with subtle folk panels in saffron at chest center only — do not overdecorate the black
WINGS (the showpiece — long fingered): folk feather bars in indigo with the primary feathers rendered as distinct elongated folk-pattern 'fingers'
TAIL: fan-shaped folk-bar pattern with fine barring
CERE + LEGS: yellow rendered as folk-gold panels with vermillion ankle bands
TALONS: prominent yellow legs and feet with black hooked claws as folk-black accents

anatomical_count_constraints.wings: 1 visible in profile (LONG with FINGERED primaries — mandatory shape)
anatomical_count_constraints.primary_fingers: prominent splayed primary feathers (mandatory in flight pose)

animals.conservation_note: Anamalai's wet evergreen canopy is prime Black Eagle territory — a nest-robber that specializes on the eggs and chicks of canopy birds, making it a top-down regulator of canopy avian communities. Most easily seen gliding slowly above the treeline.

animals.signature_features: massive eagle with DARK BROWN BODY and the unmistakable GOLDEN-BUFF NAPE (the 'golden' feathers that give the species its name — extending from the crown down the back of the neck, mandatory diagnostic), broad powerful wings, long broad tail, yellow cere and feet, dark hooked bill, golden-amber eye. Among the largest eagles of the Northern Hemisphere.
animals.signature_action: soaring at high altitude over a Ladakh ridge with wings held in a shallow V, golden nape catching sun, broad wings spread fully
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a rocky pinnacle with wings folded, head turned to scan, the golden nape feathers visible from behind
animals.anatomy_must_include: DARK BROWN BODY with GOLDEN-BUFF NAPE feathers (mandatory diagnostic — without the golden nape this could be any large dark eagle); MASSIVE body size; BROAD POWERFUL WINGS; LONG BROAD TAIL; yellow cere and feet; GOLDEN-AMBER eye; the result must read as GOLDEN EAGLE specifically, never a juvenile spotted eagle or other Aquila.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN + NAPE (the showpiece): the golden-buff nape preserved as folk-gold + saffron panel cascading from crown down the neck — mandatory diagnostic
FACE: folk-line eye accent with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BELLY: dark brown body anchored with folk panels in indigo + saffron, leaf-vein motifs
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron, broad and powerful
TAIL: long folk-bar pattern with broad bands
CERE + LEGS: yellow rendered as folk-gold panels with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.golden_nape: buff-gold feathers from crown to upper back (mandatory)

animals.conservation_note: Apex avian predator of Ladakh's trans-Himalayan cold desert; takes hares, marmots, and small ungulate calves. Hemis National Park's ridges support multiple breeding pairs. Sacred to many Himalayan Buddhist traditions.

animals.signature_features: tall elegant crane with PURE-WHITE BODY contrasting against JET-BLACK HEAD AND NECK (the unmistakable diagnostic — the BLACK NECK is mandatory and the species' identifier), BLACK FLIGHT FEATHERS at wing-tips and tail, BARE RED SKIN around the eye and crown, long black legs, long pointed dark bill. Wintering visitor to Ladakh from the Tibetan plateau.
animals.signature_action: performing the elegant pair-bonding dance: head thrown back, neck arched skyward, wings half-raised, calling — the iconic crane display
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing in a high-altitude wetland with body upright, long neck held in elegant S-curve, head turned to display the black neck and red crown
animals.anatomy_must_include: PURE WHITE BODY (mandatory contrast); JET-BLACK HEAD AND NECK (mandatory — extends from crown through entire neck to upper breast); BLACK FLIGHT FEATHERS at wing tips and tail (mandatory diagnostic — visible in flight and at rest); BARE RED SKIN on crown and around eye (mandatory); LONG BLACK LEGS; LONG POINTED DARK BILL; the result must read as BLACK-NECKED CRANE specifically (the only crane species with this exact black-on-white pattern + red crown).
required_decoration_zones: CROWN: red bare-skin patch preserved as folk-vermillion panel with saffron border
HEAD + NECK (black zone): mandatory black neck preserved as folk-black anchor with subtle vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BODY (white zone): pure white body preserved as folk-cream panel with vermillion + saffron rosette accents at chest center only
WINGS (white with black tips): folk feather bars in cream with the BLACK FLIGHT FEATHERS preserved as folk-black tips and primary edges
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern with black flight feathers preserved
LEGS: long black rendered as folk-indigo panels with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.wings: 1 visible in profile (white with black tips)

animals.conservation_note: Ladakh's high-altitude wetlands (including Hemis-associated valleys) are critical wintering habitat for this Tibetan-plateau endemic. Sacred in Buddhist tradition — the species is celebrated annually in local cultural festivals.

animals.signature_features: Manas population of the Bengal Florican — same species as the Kaziranga entry (#60): JET-BLACK HEAD/NECK/UNDERPARTS in male contrasting with PURE-WHITE WINGS, drab brown female. The Manas population is one of the two largest in the world alongside Kaziranga, set in the park's contiguous tall grassland on the Bhutan border.
animals.signature_action: male in 'parachute' courtship display, leaping vertical from tall terai grass with white wings flashing fully open
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing alert in tall grass with body vertical, head erect, legs visible, wings folded close
animals.anatomy_must_include: MALE: JET-BLACK head/neck/underparts; PURE-WHITE WINGS in display; long legs; small unmarked head; the result must read as BENGAL FLORICAN — Manas variant matches Kaziranga variant in body grammar with renderer freedom to vary the rest-pose context (Manas tall-grassland with foothills vs Kaziranga floodplain).
required_decoration_zones: HEAD + NECK (black zone): folk-black anchor with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BODY (black zone): folk panels in muted saffron + vermillion at chest center only
WINGS (white zone): folk-cream panels with leaf-vein motifs and vermillion accent at wing tips
BACK: folk-stripe pattern where wing meets body
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern
LEGS: long folk-yellow panels with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: Manas hosts one of the two largest Bengal Florican populations alongside Kaziranga; the cross-border Royal Manas (Bhutan) + Manas (India) protected complex is critical to the species' survival.

animals.signature_features: massive stork (1.5m tall — among the world's largest flying birds) with a NEARLY BALD PINKISH-GREY HEAD AND NECK (more naked than the lesser adjutant), HUGE YELLOW DAGGER BILL (largest of any Indian bird), PENDULOUS ORANGE-PINK NECK POUCH (the inflatable gular sac that distinguishes it from the lesser adjutant — mandatory diagnostic), dark grey-black back and wings, dirty WHITE BELLY, long pink-orange legs.
animals.signature_action: standing tall at a refuse pile or wetland edge with neck pouch fully inflated, bill open, wings half-spread
animals.rest_pose_for_species: hunched on a tall tree branch with neck retracted, pouch deflated and dangling, bill tucked down
animals.anatomy_must_include: NEARLY BALD PINKISH-GREY HEAD AND NECK (mandatory — more bald than lesser-adjutant); HUGE YELLOW DAGGER BILL (mandatory — the biggest of any Indian bird); PENDULOUS ORANGE-PINK NECK POUCH (mandatory diagnostic — distinguishes greater from lesser adjutant); DIRTY-WHITE BELLY; LONG PINK-ORANGE LEGS; the result must read as GREATER ADJUTANT (massive + bald + pendulous-pouched) not lesser adjutant.
required_decoration_zones: HEAD + NECK (bald pink-grey): folk-cream panel with sparse small dot 'down' marks; vermillion accent at the bill base
NECK POUCH (the diagnostic): pendulous orange-pink pouch rendered as a folk-vermillion + saffron pouch with leaf-vein motifs — mandatory to distinguish from lesser-adjutant
BILL: massive yellow dagger with gold-leaf folk accent
BREAST + BACK (dark zone): deep-indigo body with leaf-vein motifs
WHITE BELLY: preserved as folk-cream panel with vermillion border
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + cream
LEGS: long pink-orange rendered as folk-vermillion panels with cream ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (set in bald skin)
anatomical_count_constraints.neck_pouch: 1 pendulous orange-pink (mandatory diagnostic — distinguishes greater from lesser adjutant)

animals.conservation_note: Fewer than 1200 mature individuals globally — Manas + Kaziranga + Brahmaputra valley (Assam) hold roughly 80% of the world's population. Once common across South and Southeast Asia, now restricted to a few last strongholds.

animals.signature_features: huge dark-grey heron (one of the world's largest) with PURE-WHITE BELLY and PURE-WHITE THROAT and FRONT-OF-NECK STRIPE contrasting against the dark slate-grey body, LONG YELLOW DAGGER BILL, long dark legs, dark eye, slim neck plumes when in breeding plumage. Critically endangered — fewer than 250 mature individuals globally.
animals.signature_action: stalking through a fast-flowing Himalayan river with body horizontal and neck stretched, dagger bill poised over the water
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing motionless on a midstream boulder with neck retracted into shoulders, white belly visible against the dark body
animals.anatomy_must_include: DARK SLATE-GREY UPPERPARTS (mandatory); PURE-WHITE BELLY and PURE-WHITE THROAT-AND-FRONT-NECK STRIPE (mandatory contrast — the white-on-grey is THE diagnostic); LONG YELLOW DAGGER BILL; LONG DARK LEGS; the result must read as WHITE-BELLIED HERON specifically (the rarest of the world's heron species) not great-blue heron, not grey heron.
required_decoration_zones: CROWN + FACE: dark slate panel with folk-line eye accent
WHITE THROAT + FRONT NECK: preserved as folk-cream stripe running from chin down the front of the neck (mandatory diagnostic — never omit)
BREAST + BACK (dark slate zone): deep-indigo body anchored with folk panels in subtle saffron
WHITE BELLY: preserved as folk-cream panel with vermillion border
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo with cream stripe accents
BILL: yellow dagger with folk-gold accent
LEGS: long dark rendered as folk-indigo panels with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.white_belly_and_throat_stripe: mandatory diagnostic (white throat + white belly + white front-neck stripe)

animals.conservation_note: Fewer than 250 mature individuals globally. Namdapha's fast-flowing pristine rivers (along with adjacent Bhutan and Myanmar) hold the bulk of the world's surviving population. One of Asia's most threatened bird species.

animals.signature_features: spectacular Eastern Himalayan hornbill, MALE has RUFOUS-CHESTNUT HEAD AND NECK (mandatory diagnostic) and chestnut breast contrasting with BLACK BACK and WHITE WING-TIPS, modest yellow-and-red bill with small reddish casque on top, BRIGHT BLUE BARE FACIAL SKIN around the eye, white tail with broad black band. Female has all-black head and neck instead of rufous.
animals.signature_action: in flight with broad wings fully spread showing the white wing-tips, rufous head and neck prominent, casque held forward
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a high canopy branch with bill held forward, both feet visible, body upright
animals.anatomy_must_include: MALE: RUFOUS-CHESTNUT HEAD AND NECK extending to chestnut breast (mandatory diagnostic — never plain black, never plain brown); BLACK BACK contrasting with rufous chest; WHITE WING-TIPS visible in flight (mandatory); BRIGHT BLUE FACIAL SKIN around eye; modest casque (NOT massive yellow like great-hornbill); the result must read as RUFOUS-NECKED HORNBILL (Eastern Himalayan endemic) not Great Hornbill, not Wreathed Hornbill.
required_decoration_zones: HEAD + NECK (rufous-chestnut zone): folk-pattern panel in saffron + vermillion with leaf-vein motifs (mandatory color anchor for the male)
CASQUE: small reddish casque with folk-vermillion + gold-leaf accent
BILL: yellow-and-red bill with folk-pattern bands
FACE: blue facial-skin patch preserved as folk-indigo panel around vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BACK (black zone): forest-teal body fill with folk panels in saffron + leaf-green at chest
WINGS: folk feather bars with WHITE WING-TIPS preserved as cream tips flanked by vermillion
TAIL: folk-bar pattern with WHITE TAIL preserved and a BROAD BLACK BAND across
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (in blue facial skin)
anatomical_count_constraints.rufous_neck: rufous-chestnut head and neck (mandatory in males)

animals.conservation_note: Namdapha is one of the last strongholds in India for this Eastern Himalayan endemic; population threatened by canopy loss and hunting throughout its range. Among the most range-restricted Indian hornbills.

animals.signature_features: beautifully patterned ground bird — male has CHESTNUT-BROWN BODY covered with WHITE SPOTS each finely RINGED WITH BLACK (the iconic 'painted' pattern), GLOSSY BLACK HEAD AND BREAST contrasting with the spotted body, RED BARE FACIAL SKIN around eye, RED-PURPLE LEGS with prominent spurs (males), dark hooked bill. The intricate white-on-chestnut spotting is the spectacle.
animals.signature_action: male in display: head held high with red facial skin prominent, body puffed showing the patterned spotting, tail slightly raised
animals.rest_pose_for_species: standing on rocky scrub ground with body horizontal, both feet visible, head turned to display the spotted plumage
animals.anatomy_must_include: MALE: CHESTNUT-BROWN BODY with WHITE-AND-BLACK-RINGED SPOTS (mandatory 'painted' pattern — the spots are THE diagnostic); GLOSSY BLACK HEAD AND BREAST (mandatory contrast); RED FACIAL SKIN around eye; RED-PURPLE LEGS with SPURS visible (mandatory in males); the result must read as PAINTED SPURFOWL specifically (intricately patterned, endemic to peninsular India), distinct from francolins.
required_decoration_zones: HEAD + BREAST (glossy black zone): folk-black panel preserved with vermillion eye ring around the red facial skin
FACE: red facial skin preserved as folk-vermillion panel

BODY (chestnut + ringed-spots — the showpiece): the chestnut body covered with WHITE-RINGED-WITH-BLACK SPOTS translated as folk-ornament — each spot a small folk-cream circle with a folk-black border ring, arranged rhythmically across the entire body and flanks
BACK + WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron with the spotted pattern continuing
TAIL: short folk-bar pattern
LEGS: red-purple rendered as folk-vermillion panels with SPURS visible as folk-saffron projections at the base of each ankle (mandatory in males)

anatomical_count_constraints.eyes_visible: 1 in side profile (in red facial skin)
anatomical_count_constraints.ringed_spots: many WHITE-with-BLACK-ring spots across body (mandatory pattern)

animals.conservation_note: Endemic to peninsular India. Sariska's rocky scrubland and Aravalli outcrops provide ideal habitat. Less commonly seen than its grey francolin cousin due to its preference for dense thorn-scrub cover.

animals.signature_features: open-country hornbill (NOT a forest hornbill — palette anchors to deep-indigo not forest-teal), with PROMINENT POINTED CASQUE on a curved YELLOW-ORANGE BILL (the casque has a forward-pointing tip — mandatory diagnostic), GREY-BROWN BODY with paler underparts, WHITE TAIL TIPS, dark eye, slim athletic profile distinct from the larger forest hornbills. Common across dry plains of central and northern India.
animals.signature_action: in flight with bill held forward, casque prominent, broad rounded wings spread, characteristic dipping flight pattern
animals.rest_pose_for_species: perched on a babool or thorn-tree branch with body upright, casque prominent in profile, both feet visible
animals.anatomy_must_include: PROMINENT POINTED CASQUE on top of curved bill (mandatory — the forward-pointing tip is the visual signature); YELLOW-ORANGE BILL; GREY-BROWN BODY (mandatory — never the bold black-and-white of Malabar Pied, never the chestnut-and-black of Rufous-necked); WHITE TAIL TIPS; slim profile; the result must read as INDIAN GREY HORNBILL (the open-country hornbill of northern India) not Malabar Grey Hornbill (which has a smaller casque and Western Ghats distribution).
required_decoration_zones: CASQUE: prominent pointed casque with folk-yellow + saffron accents and a folk-line ridge
BILL: yellow-orange curved bill with folk-pattern bands
FACE: folk-line eye accent with vermillion eye ring
BREAST + BODY (grey-brown): deep-indigo body fill with folk panels in saffron + leaf-green at chest
WINGS: folk feather bars in indigo + saffron
TAIL: folk-bar pattern with WHITE TAIL TIPS preserved as cream banding
FEET: both visible with vermillion ankle bands
animals.conservation_note: Common across Sariska's dry-deciduous Aravalli landscape. A generalist that has adapted well to mixed agricultural-and-forest landscapes; one of the most easily seen hornbills in northern India.

_collaborator_notes: v2.0.0 catalog: 40 animals across 20 Indian national parks, two flagship species per park, organized as a single 'indian' series. This is the deliberate foundation for a Madhubani T-shirt collection and an eventual LoRA fine-tune. Every entry MUST include: slug, display_name, binomial, series, park, park_state, ordinal_in_series, body_type, body_fill_color, body_fill_color_name, signature_features, signature_action, rest_pose_for_species, iucn_status, conservation_note, seed_block_start. The optional anatomy_must_include field SHOULD be added for any species with strong pretrained signals likely to override our folk-art register (big cats whose natural orange/tawny coloring competes with the walnut anchor; species with diagnostic field marks like the rhino's single horn, the sloth bear's chest blaze, the red panda's ringed tail, the Irrawaddy dolphin's beakless head, the skimmer's elongated lower mandible). seed_block_start values are unique 100-step increments within the indigo seed block (8200-12200); see the per-entry values for actual allocation. Body fill follows the v2 convention: deep-indigo #1a2952 as default; walnut-brown #5a3a1f for big cats and sloth bear AND red panda AND indian fox (warm-fur species); forest-teal #1f4a3f for forest snakes and forest hornbills. Naturally colorful species (peacock, sarus crane, painted stork, greater flamingo, golden langur, nilgiri langur, red panda, indian giant squirrel) keep their species-specific colors on inner folk panels over a fundamental anchor body fill — they do NOT become uniformly indigo. The african and bird series from v1.0.0 are deprecated and preserved only as historical record in _deprecated_series.
